item.content:encoded:
I made a place for lonely hearts to find
It's where I wait for you.

I'm going to stay until the end has come
And make my peace with you.

Maybe this is just an old deceit
Maybe it's the truth.

-Polybius (James Houston)

item.title: The tags
item.content:encoded: Maybe consider adding the 'sentient' tag?

item.title: I guess they would be pissed off...
item.content:encoded:
… that they were not deployed to the evac mission in SCP-1730 as firepower backup to Samsara. Would be the game of their lives - it was basically a huge horror-themed Quake-like map anyway; lots of non-human monsters to kill, all humans are civilians or friendlies so you have to protect and not kill them, a far easier pill to swallow; *and* a reality-bending machine that might give them some interesting chances.

The Doylist reason, of course, is that SCP-1730 predates this article.

When I think of this scenario I also wonder about something - are they able to interact with their surroundings on mission at all, other than firepower? Are they able to coordinate with another MTF such as Samsara?

(Were they to meet Samsara, it would be unkillable digital gods meet just unkillable gods…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorites. Just one question. If humans look like quake enemies, what do other animals or scps look like?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Loading map. We're going in."

AMAZING.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Just wow.

Really enjoyed this one. It's dark (definitely fills the Foundation quota) but not hopeless. I quite like how the group developed into a MTF. Reminds me a lot of MTF Tau-5 "Samsara"

Would love to see a tale starring these guys. No idea how that would be handled, but hey, a guy can dream, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how they're an MTF now, hopefully we see them more. They're like samsara the number of which I can't remember atm

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's mentioned in SCP-3797, which is linked by that line. There's no actual file for it, yes, but it's referenced in 3797. Omega-9 assisted with 3797-ARC but not 3797.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Loading map. We're going in."

Fk yeah. That's it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the last few logs and the happy ending. Regardless, this is a great skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3797-ARC seems to not exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate to you. Xd
I started reading. 'oh. Generic videogame-is-real thing. Lame'
Scrolled down. 'and tons of collapsy-things. Tempted to downvote and move on'

But decided it was unfair. Decided to read the chat logs at least.
By the time of 'they said yes', I fistpumped. I was rooting for them to get to be useful. XD
Upvoted, for sure!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read the translated version first. I loved it.+1
Read this original version next. I TOTALLY love this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: THIS IS AWESOME!! +1!!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is a good change. Let it be.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Another possible way to add a crosslink to SCP-3797 would be to format it similarly to the main task forces page, making a bold section for "Assisting In Containment of Objects:" and listing SCP-3797 under that. (Possibly formatting it as SCP-3797-ARC, in that case.) This would allow for more room in the future if other authors used Omega-9, allowing for them to also be added.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I instantly approve of this. In fact, if I didn't happen to be following this SCP's discussion, I wouldn't have even realized there was a new Omega-9 article! Time to go read now, I guess~!

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, spoiler alert, but this article ends with the anomaly in question becoming an MTF. Another article on the site uses that same MTF as part of it's containment. That article, SCP-3797. It doesn't seem too far a stretch to link from here to there as part of the continuation of the story. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Mobile Task Force Omega-9 consists of 3 anomalous entities available for near-instantaneous deployment into extreme circumstances requiring the delivery of overwhelming force. Its primary purpose is as a rapid-response team for containment breaches involving violent, hostile anomalies. As part of the agreement reached with these 3 entities, MTF Omega-9 is only to be used to eliminate hostile non-human targets.

To this:

Mobile Task Force Omega-9 consists of 3 anomalous entities available for near-instantaneous deployment into extreme circumstances requiring the delivery of overwhelming force. Its primary purpose is as a rapid-response team for containment breaches involving violent, hostile anomalies. As part of the agreement reached with these 3 entities, MTF Omega-9 is only to be used to eliminate hostile non-human targets.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can find all his articles on his author page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, I am brand-spanking-new to the SCP wiki (well, I've read SCP's before, but just got an account) and holy crap I loved this. Like seriously, I adored it. The characters were extremely believable, the premise was interesting, and it had such a nice conclusion. One downside is that, reading these comments, I see people praising the author for just being a great SCP writer. I got excited, until I noticed that they are now listed as "Account Deleted". Guess I'll just have to hope I stumble across another one of these by "Hippo", whoever they were…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There aren't any, atm; it's not something I or anyone else has written about. The article's still pretty new!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God, that is awesome. I can imagine the political tension on that very 05 meeting. I guess we just had to see this from the SCPs' perspective, though, where they wouldn't be able to see what's happening behind the closed doors.

Also, I couldn't find it, where can I see more tales of this MTF?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Part of the acceptance of this arrangement is, in itself, a containment procedure. SCP-2639 is overwhelmingly powerful; they can manifest wherever they want. The O5 council could easily destroy the computer they're running, but they don't want to do that if they don't have to.

Giving SCP-2639 what it wants — letting them be a productive part of the Foundation — is how the Foundation contains SCP-2639. If they're helping, they're less likely to cause damage to the Foundation.

I imagine this was a very contentious decision, and passed by a very close vote. But I also imagine that there's an explosive charge attached to that computer — ready to be detonated at the press of a button.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 and LOVE it!

One thing, the O5 accepted too early to allow an SCP to become an MTF, with the failure of Pandora's Box and all. I'm thinking there would be a ton of resistance to the idea even after SCP-2639 saved a life and dealt with a containment breach. I think there just needs to be more reason given to accept them in. Even as I was reading this article, I was so certain the idea was going to get rejected, til I saw it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been so depressed lately I barely ever smile even at things I enjoy. I smiled wide at that ending. It's nice when things work out. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God damn Hippo, your skips are good. +1, yet again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic. The game, the setup, the logs… you're a genius, Hippo.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is amazing and I love it, it's one of my favorite skips now! Take my upvote—I'd give this ten upvotes, if I could!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In every way, this works for me. I don't even have useful feedback, just take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see omega 9 as a combination of alpha 9 and omega 7 lol

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: +1000000 to that logo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn do I love this one badly.

Omega-9.

[WTF_STFU] bs
[WTF_STFU] yes you can
[WTF_STFU] make the call
[WTF_STFU] we'll follow your lead
[JBREINER] PLEASE HELP ITS BREAKING DOWN THE DOOR
[GRRGRL] Loading map. We're going in.

Fing chills reading that. So awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is the badass MTF Omega-9 that's been popping up while I catch up on too many gosh darned entries. And I have to say, this is one HELL of an origin story for them. I like the chat log style entries. You use them in a way I think Andrew Hussie would be jealous of. Love it, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a ton of fun. There's a power fantasy here, but one that's relatable and tempered by quality existential horror. The cheesiness of the ending is somehow just right; it tied everything together perfectly, and just appealed to the teenage FPS player I used to be. This is a happy ending done right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What can I say? This one is awesome.

The emotional roller coaster is what captured my attention. The grim realization when they realised that they killed actual people, the determination when they decide to aid Foundation during the breach, they are all very well written. Especially when they are just kids.

And as someone mentioned, the "They said yes" sent chills down my spine as well.

A very hearty +1.

Edit: tale idea. Death match with 076 or 682 perhaps? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing says that theyll be voiped away forever into darkness afterward. Maybe they'll just disconnect and flop back into existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Is this fucking ReBoot? This is fucking stupid, why is it +123? I mean maybe its fun to read but… Ugh theres so much. Whatever sure I'll read it people say Hippos a great author I'll give it a shot."

\> Omega-9

"Sorry, +124."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, massively impressed. Great job writing the characters.

+1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would the Scrubs handle containment breaches without accidentally killing foundation personnel?

They probably would only be deployed when there weren't any personnel around to kill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda wanna see these guys contain 106.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted at the begining. "Too game-ish" - I thought. But something puled me to read more.
And at the end i upvoted as hard as i can.
Maybe it sound too stupid, but i wanna read combined operation of Alpha and Omega niners.
Think it would be great victory from O5 perspective, but no one in both MTF will not enjoy it.

Thanks for greate reading!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A question: How would the Scrubs handle containment breaches without accidentally killing foundation personnel?

Really like this, by the way. Refreshing to see a good ending every now and then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The mixed feels in this. It goes in a direction I thought we were trying to move away from. But it works. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate it — that means a lot coming from you!

And yeah, this was one of those things where the premise wasn't working because it was way too boring — until one day I was struck by the notion of it being Thaumiel (and immediately infatuated by the story of how that could possibly happen).

I was (and still am) concerned that the opening is too long and risks turning readers away — if I edited anything about this article, it would be making the opening shorter (possibly take away the mention of power ups) so as to get to the actual story faster.

ETA: Also I know they're not Thaumiel technically, but the 'idea' of the story came from the question 'what if they were Thaumiel' — just to clarify >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd echo all of Rioghail's first paragraph. Turning around my initial dubiousness about the concept is down to strong execution and some decent character work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They said yes.

Chills down my spine man. Hippo, you're a genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! xD

I actually got 'shitlord' from Nesticle, an NES emulator — which was released in 97. Here's a screenshot of its 'About' page (warning for grossness!): BOW TO THE FECAL-LORD. Not the same as 'shitlord', but that image was the genesis for the term in my head >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, what an opinion rollercoaster. By the time I'd finished the description I felt reasonably sure I wasn't going to get much out of this article - I've seen the 'but the anomaly's actually a video game!' trope used too many times and thought this was going to be a retread of a bunch of things I'd seen before. The initial set of logs took this in a direction I wasn't expecting and were really interesting (I particularly like Booger's swerve from 'boot the shitlord' to 'holy shit i can't see my hands'). I like Reiner pleading with them to stop the breach. And while the change to utilising them as an MTF is pretty campy, it didn't stop me having a massive cheesy grin on my face when I saw where it was going.

One thing I would point out is that some of the dialog is pretty anachronistic for teens who've had no outside contact since 1997 - the one that stuck out to me was 'shitlord', which as far as I can tell wasn't used until the late 2000s, but I wouldn't be surprised if there are other words which have crept in erroneously from modern-day internet slang.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No worries; I totally get this being not to your taste — I appreciate you saying it's well-written, nevertheless!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this is a well written article in its own right, I fail to be able take this seriously. The whole concept takes itself way too seriously for what it is and there's no nuance regarding the last few logs. It's essentially a sob story about these three friends trapped in real-life Quake with a happy end. It just doesn't do it for me.

Like I said though, this is a well written article in its own right. As there's nothing inherently wrong or personally disagreeable with it, this is a no-vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and in 20 years, when the computer is finally too worn out to keep going and it shuts off…

… all three of them will rematerialize, exactly as they were in 1997.

If only it had been shut down back then. If only the Foundation had turned it off.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: You're welcome!

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: User Tao McCawley made the following logo for MTF Omega-9; added it to the article. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember when Peter Gabriel was edgy. X3
Good times, good times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! And that was a huge worry of mine in writing this (that it ends up becoming a power fantasy); my hope was that the sheer shittiness of their situation would offset that — yeah, they're basically fucking unkillable BATTLE-GODS, but they'd much rather just be teenagers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though this is very similar to an Able style power fantasy, I just can't help but love it. You somehow managed to make it work. Cheers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although the difference between goth and emocore wasn't very distinct at least in clubs here, until later, when it seems to be mostly generational, IME (e.g I've seen Bauhaus live and was there when we sacked Rome, you damned kids).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, it dates back to the late 80s: Emocore was the intersection of Dark Wave and Hardcore Punk, and the contraction "emo" got genericized first into the name for the fans of said music, and then into "brooding teens and young adults who weren't goths or grunge fans".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
? 'Emo' was being used as early as the 80s: link!

(It might not have been getting used in the way that WTF_STFU uses it, though. I THINK it has, but I might be wrong)

ETA: Oops; ninja'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you mean the exact literal word, no: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emo First use dates back to 1985. If you're talking about the fashion/subculture, then it probably is a weird term to use in 97; the specific culture it references came about around early 00s.
